entry.title: HANA Sizing Keypoints
entry.content:
SAP HANA sizing is a crucial step in preparing your system for both current business needs and future growth. Whether you’re planning for Suite on SAP HANA or transitioning to S/4HANA, getting the sizing right ensures optimal performance, cost-efficiency, and scalability. This blog breaks down key aspects of SAP HANA sizing, complete with easy-to-follow formulas and examples.

1. Data Volume Sizing: Planning for Growth

Data sizing is the backbone of your SAP HANA setup. It ensures there’s enough space for your data today and accounts for growth in the years to come. SAP recommends the following formula:

Key Factors:

  • Net Data Volume on Disk: The current size of your data on disk. For example, this might be 149.7 GiB based on your analysis.
  • Factor 1.2: Adds a 20% buffer for operations like Delta Merge.
  • Year-Over-Year (YoY) Growth Factor: Accounts for expected growth. For instance, 15% annual growth over three years gives a factor of 1.52=(1.15)31.52 = (1.15)^31.52=(1.15)3.

This formula not only ensures there’s enough space but also prepares your system for seamless future expansions.

2. Log Volume Sizing: Securing Data Integrity

Log volumes are critical for maintaining database integrity during savepoints and backups. The size of the log volume depends on the amount of RAM and system workload. Use the following formula:

 

​

 

Examples:

  • For a system with 128 GB RAM, log volume = 64 GB.
  • For a system with 512 GB RAM, log volume = 256 GB.
  • For a system with 1 TB RAM or more, the minimum recommended log volume is 512 GB.

It’s essential to account for higher workloads or delays in savepoints, which might require additional log volume space to prevent issues.

3. Installation Disk Space: Keeping It Simple

When planning your disk space for SAP HANA installation, you should allocate enough space for /hana/shared/<SID>. For single-node installations, the formula is straightforward:

Examples:

  • A system with 128 GB RAM → Installation Size = 128 GB.
  • A system with 1 TB RAM → Installation Size = 1 TB.
  • A system with 2 TB RAM → Installation Size = 1 TB (maximum limit for single-node).

For scale-out systems, calculate the installation size based on the combined requirements of all nodes.

4. Disk Space for Backups: Ensuring Safety

Backups are your safety net. You need sufficient space to store both full and delta backups. Here’s the formula:

​

Backup Cycle:

  • For a typical 28-day backup cycle:
    • Weekends: Full backups.
    • Workdays: Delta backups.

Having adequate backup space ensures smooth operations, even during emergencies or system failures.

5. Export Disk Space: Preparing for Troubleshooting

In some cases, like root cause analysis, you might need to export database content. SAP recommends reserving space equivalent to twice the size of the largest table in your database:

This ensures that even in critical situations, you have the resources to perform a detailed analysis.

6. Network Requirements: Keeping It Fast and Reliable

For SAP HANA, your network plays a critical role in ensuring fast communication between components. Here are the minimum bandwidth requirements:

  • Internal and Storage Zones: ≥ 10 Gb Ethernet (GbE) or ≥ 8 Gb Fibre Channel (GbF).
  • Proper segmentation of networks is also recommended, separating client, internal, storage, and admin zones for better performance and security.

7. CPU Sizing: Planning for User Load

SAP HANA CPU sizing depends on the number of active users and their workload. Use this formula for a rough estimate:

​

For example, a system with 200 active users would need approximately:

This estimate ensures your system can handle user workloads without exceeding 65% CPU utilization.

Conclusion

Sizing SAP HANA isn’t just about crunching numbers—it’s about future-proofing your system for growth and ensuring seamless performance. By following these formulas and best practices, you can confidently size your SAP HANA setup for today’s demands and tomorrow’s challenges.

References

  1. SAP HANA Master Guide
    SAP HANA Master Guide

  2. SAP Notes

    • SAP Note 1872170: ABAP on HANA Sizing Report
    • SAP Note 2407186: High Availability Guides.
    • SAP Note 1900823: SAP HANA Storage Connector API.
    • SAP Note 2786237: Persistent Memory Sizing.
    • SAP Note 1793345: Sizing for SAP Suite on SAP HANA.
    • SAP Note 1637145: SAP BW on HANA Sizing.

  3. SAP White Papers

    • SAP HANA Network Requirements

By keeping these guidelines in mind, you can set up your SAP HANA system to not just function but thrive.


Source:https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/installing-and-administering-sap-hana/performing-sap-hana-sizing

Liked it? Don't forget to give a kudos! or atleast feedback, will improvise

Happy Learning

#HANA, #/SDF/HDB_SIZING

entry.title: Re: Versioning in Analytic Data Model (SAC)
entry.content:
@Artur3 

I am not sure at what stage you got the error but Let me clarify few things.

You can have multiple versions Like Actual, Plan, Budget, Forecast in Analytic Model and also load the data to it. The restriction is that you cannot have more than one version with version category set as Actuals as shown below

But As you can see I have a version called PL with category set as Planning and you can load data to this version without any issues.

While mapping the file just assign the version PL or any version which you have in your model as shown below.

Hope this helps !!

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Deployment Error : 403 error in Fiori App deployed in...
entry.content:
Hi Arasu,

I would suggest you check out the blog post Enterprise Resource Planning Blogs by SAP. There especially the "Decision matrix – When to use which extensibility option?". If your target group are Employees who use S/4HANA all day long is I see this options:

  1. Use a Custom Tile to point to the BTP Frontend
  2. Publish S/4HANA to SAP Build Work Zone (Content Providers - Cloud Solutions) and integrate the BTP Frontend there as an additional app
  3. Develop your extenstion on stack using Embedded Steampunk

Best regards,
Gregor

entry.title: Re: The “onAfterDataEntryProcess” event in SAP Analytics Cloud Optimized Story
entry.content:
@SarauShen 

Thanks for the writeup. Although this is a great step in the right direction, I think this would be too early to compare this with BPC Default logic. The context of changed data was managed by itself in BPC default logic. As a developer we never had to read the context process them using script and then pass it as parameter to DA. Also BPC Default logic would get triggered for all forms of data entry including data loads /journal entries/input forms/excel based data entry. This solution would limit us to only Table in Optimized story. Also I would love to know how large a data change context will this method support and what will be the performance in case of large data set changes.

Thanks

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: How to Connect SAP Build Apps to Demo S/4HANA Data
entry.content:
You can connect any REST/OData API, and through a destination you handle any type of authentication to those APIs that destinations support. For more complex scenarios Integration Suite (API Management) can be used.

I'm not familiar with how you created the API and what authentication it requires so I couldn't say specifically. I've created CDS views and connected to them with basic authentication, but you likely why to create trust between your BTP and your S/4 system, and that's beyond my expertise.

entry.title: Re: Using Apache Kafka for messaging in the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) – Part 1/2
entry.content:
Hi @vobu,

that's some really interesting news. I know that (Advanced) Event Mesh is based on Solace, but if SAP Cloud Application Event Hub is using Kafka as the internal message broker, I am really curious on why SAP made this decision. Especially since Event Hub seems to thought of as the default broker for BTP and SAP cloud apps.

Regarding the direct access to Kafka: I don't think it's possible to directly connect to the underlying broker, since SAP Cloud Application Event Hub adds a big application layer on top and messages are delivered to subscribing applications via HTTP, not any messaging protocol.

But I will add some example use cases for cds-kafka in the second post. To bring in even more confusion in the bouquet of message broker options. 😀

entry.title: Re: reference information missing or data type wrong usin...
entry.content:
Hello, is that field a quantity field? a currency field? 

I will suppose that MON1 field is a quantity field.

You will need to: 

  • To add a base unit of measure in the CDS view
    • let's give "as BaseUnit" an alias of this new field
  • To link the “BaseUnit” of measure to the quantity field with this annotation: @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'BaseUnit'

Thus your cds view will look like this: 

{...

prart_des5 as PrartDes5,

prart_des6 as PrartDes6,

@Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'BaseUnit'

mon_1 as Mon1,

...

}

This will make the error disappear

entry.title: Re: Managing replication flow Delta load dependency on ne...
entry.content:
You need to leverage the CLI and OS Scripting.

Execute the flow via TaskChain, then get the log and based on the result, trigger next TaskChain, or failure Email. You will need to write a script - wrapper scheduled like every 1-5 minutes checking if the one or other TaskChains run and in what status it is in.

entry.title: Re: MTB build failure after upgraded to npm 11
entry.content:
Hello,

when you use cf buildpacks to get the supported build packs by BTP you find:

5          nodejs_buildpack                   cflinuxfs4   true      false    READY   nodejs_buildpack-cached-cflinuxfs4-v1.8.29.zip

At:

https://github.com/cloudfoundry/nodejs-buildpack/releases/tag/v1.8.29

you will find the supported 20.x Node.JS Versions. The latest one 20.16.0 is 18.20.3. It's npm version seems to be  10.8.1 according to:

https://nodejs.org/en/blog/release/v20.16.0

your engines entry:

"npm": "11.0.0"

will not work for this.

entry.title: Differences in SAP configuration steps required for Build-to-Order (BTO) compared to MTO or ATO?
entry.content:
I have implemented MTO (Make-to-Order) and ATO (Assemble-to-Order). I used planning strategy 20 for MTO and 81/82 for ATO.
I am unsure how BTO (Build-to-Order) differs from ATO or MTO, or if it's the same thing. The key differences I've identified are:
  • MTO is the broadest term, encompassing all production triggered by a customer order.
     
  • BTO emphasizes customization and configuration during the production process.
     
  • ATO involves a combination of pre-production and final assembly based on customer orders.
What different SAP configuration steps are required for BTO? And how do these steps differentiate it from MTO or ATO?

entry.title: Monitoring Storage optimal usage
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

 
We are currently using the SAP Integration Suite within our organization, and it is shared among several teams. We are facing challenges with monitoring storage utilization, especially since one team might use more storage than others. The maximum allowed monitoring storage is 35,000 MB. We are also implementing pipeline concepts, which result in 3 or more messages for a single message transfer from sender to receiver.
 
Could you please provide guidance on the following:
1)How can we utilize the monitoring storage wisely and enforce best practices across all teams?
2)What are the potential risks and worst-case scenarios if we exceed the maximum allowed monitoring storage?
3)What strategies can we implement to avoid these risks, especially considering our use of pipeline concepts that generate multiple messages?
We appreciate any insights or recommendations you can offer to help us manage our monitoring storage effectively.

 

Thanks

Ramu.

entry.title: Step into the Future of Integration: Using Azure CosmosDB Receiver Adapter in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Background

With its December 27, 2024 release, SAP introduced the Azure CosmosDB receiver adapter, enhancing the SAP Integration Suite's capabilities. Azure CosmosDB, a cloud-native NoSQL and vector database from Microsoft, supports globally distributed applications with high availability and low latency. In this blog, we will explore the features of Azure CosmosDB and the new receiver adapter, walk through the setup process, and demonstrate a sample integration scenario to help you get started.

The new Azure CosmosDB receiver adapter is generally available, subject to your subscription plan. For more details, refer to SAP Notes 2903776 and 3188446. You can find it in SAP Business Accelerator Hub or directly on Discovery page of you Cloud Integration tenant.

Below I gather few links that can help you with starting up: 

Azure CosmosDB documentation: Azure Cosmos DB | Microsoft Learn

AzureCosmosDB Adapter for SAP Integration Suite: https://api.sap.com/package/AzureCosmosDBAdapterforSAPIntegrationSuite/overview 
SAP Help Page: https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/azure-cosmosdb-receiver-adapter
 

What is Azure CosmosDB? 

Azure Cosmos DB, a cloud-native database service from Microsoft, is designed to support globally distributed, modern applications. It offers high availability, exceptional scalability, and low-latency access to data. Unlike traditional relational databases, Cosmos DB is a NoSQL and vector database, making it versatile enough to handle unstructured, semi-structured, structured, and vector data.

Azure CosmosDB organizes data into 'items,' which are stored in 'containers.' The terminology and structure may vary based on the API used. For example, with the MongoDB-compatible API, data is referred to as 'documents' within 'collections'. These containers are grouped into "databases," functioning as namespaces. One standout feature of Cosmos DB is its schema-agnostic design, allowing the addition of items without requiring a predefined schema.

 

Features of Azure CosmosDB Receiver Adapter on SAP Cloud Integration

At first glance, the Azure CosmosDB adapter functions similarly to the Azure Storage adapter in SAP Integration Suite. This is because CosmosDB, on the surface, got similar integration capability as Blobs that are kept in containers. This helps a lot in understanding how potential integration scenarios and patterns can be designed. Therefore, if you are familiar with Azure Storage adapter on Integration Suite you will quickly catch how to use also this new adapter.

What is nice about Azure CosmosDB is that you don’t need to really define the data model before you start playing with DB itself or integrating with it. You just need few steps of preparation and you are ready. Just create new CosmosDB in your workspace, then create the Container in it and assign Partition Key in case it's required (in some specific scenarios it will be needed).

Azure CosmosDB adapter allows for all CRUD operations with option to List, Query and Replace records on objects in scope that are Database, Containers and Documents of CosmosDB account configured on the adapter. Documents are simply records specified by id and optionally by Partition Keys.

Partition Keys are groupings for Documents in Container, in case of Products it might be CategoryID. Partition Keys do not need to be unique across containers and documents but serve to logically group documents for improved data organization and retrieval. Apart from Single Document processing adapter provides also an option to use Bulk processing option.

With an understanding of the adapter's capabilities, let's move to the setup process required for this integration. Below show few steps needed on Azure side to start with.

 

Prerequisites and Initial Setup

1. First create an account on https://portal.azure.com/

2. Once you are done with it, request creation of CosmosDB resource in your Azure account

 

3. Creation and deployment of your first CosmosDB should go fast and result with resource available as below

 

4. Now you are ready to create your first Data Base and Container, in my case I have designed it to keep Products. For now the Items are empty but soon it will be fulfilled with data coming from SAP Cloud Integration. 

 

I didn't specified additional Partition Key, its important aspect on Azure CosmosDB, for more information about it you can refer to following documentation. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/partitioning-overview 

In general for small databases int is not required to introduce the additional partition key and you can set it to "/id" of your Document. For big data collections in CosmosDB Partition Key is required. For container with products apart of Product ID key you can introduce Partition Key as Product Category ID that will group Products logically. 

5. The final critical step is to obtain the API Key for integration and configure it in SAP Cloud Integration as a Secure Parameter under Security Materials. You have an option to use Master Key Token or Resource Token (Isolated to specific resource on Azure). In this scenario I used Master Key.

 

Here you go you can now start using your CosmosDB and Container in your integration scenario!

In the next section, we will demonstrate a simple integration scenario where data from an external API is transformed and loaded into Azure CosmosDB using the SAP Integration Suite - Cloud Integration.

 

Demo Scenario 

The demo scenario is straightforward yet sufficient to illustrate the basic steps and principles for setting up a connection and integrating with Azure CosmosDB using SAP Cloud Integration. The following section provides an overview of the iFlow built to implement this scenario.

Data of Products will be pulled from REST API of Northwind JSON Server, that is free of charge demo API with sample data for test scenarios.

Products will be pulled from Northwind API using SAP Cloud Integration iFlow started with timer (run once). Products pulled from Demo API will be transformed slightly to meet expectations of CosmosDB and processed in Bulk mode to my Azure CosmosDB. Below snippet shows part of Northwind Products payload that will be processed by the iFlow. 

Data Source: northwind.vercel.app/api/products

Although it's important to mention that apart from requirement of having unique UUID "id" or additional PartitionKey, if expected by CosmosDB, there is no restriction and no special requirement on JSON structure pushed to CosmosDB as no data model creation and modeling is expected upfront. Below is overview of an iFlow build for this scenario. 

 

Main iFlow steps: 

1. Data of Products is pulled from Northwind API

2. Transformation of Product payload to fit into CosmosDB container. The Groovy script is used to transform the Product payload to align with CosmosDB's requirements. Here's how the script works:: 

a. Introduce ProductID field and copy "id" of Product into ProductID

b. Replace id with unique UUID accepted by CosmosDB as a key for Document 

Content before groovy script processing: 

Snippet of groovy code: 

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper;
import groovy.json.JsonOutput;
import java.util.UUID;

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String);
    
    def jsonSlurper = new JsonSlurper();
    def productsList = jsonSlurper.parseText(body);

    if (productsList instanceof List) {
        productsList.each { product ->
            if (product != null) {
                if (product.id != null) {
                    product.ProductID = product.id;
                }
                product.id = UUID.randomUUID().toString();
            }
        }
    } else {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid JSON structure: Expected a list of products.");
    }

    def updatedJson = JsonOutput.toJson(productsList);

    updatedJson = JsonOutput.prettyPrint(updatedJson);

    message.setBody(updatedJson);

    return message;
}

 

The output of this Groovy script ensures that each Product has a unique UUID as the 'id' field and an additional 'ProductID' field. Result of groovy script processing: 

Main principle for Products transformation is that CosmosDB expect id of Document in a form of unique UUID and Northwind Product ID cannot be used. This code is just for a purpose of this demo, real life solution will look slightly different but requirement on CosmosDB side need to be addressed in overall design.  

3. Data of Products is pushed to Azure CosmosDB 

Configuration of Azure CosmosDB adapter is setup with Azure CosmosDB account details and Master Key Alias. 

The Processing Tab is configured to use Bulk mode, enabling us to process all products from the Northwind API at once. The Upsert mode is selected to create or update records seamlessly, ensuring that any document with the same UUID already existing in the container is updated instead of duplicated. This helps to do creation and update in one request. 

Database and Container is specified. As we dont have additional PartitionKey we are using our main table key /id as PartitionKey. 

 

Demo execution

Next, we will execute the iFlow and observe the results. Our Container in CosmosDB named "Products2" is still empty but we will fulfil it with data using our Demo iFlow. 

Our iFlow runs fine and without errors but you need be careful here as in case of Bulk processing Cloud Integration will not raise exceptions during runtime in case problems with Documents Upsert on CosmosDB side. Exception will be only raise in case of connectivity or adapter configuration issues or in case of processing Single mode set on adapter. As mentioned, I’m using Bulk operation on Documents to Upsert new records into my CosmosDB. 

Proper error handling is crucial. Note that errors on the CosmosDB side are not flagged as failures by the Azure CosmosDB adapter in Bulk mode. The processing status is marked as 'Completed' in SAP Cloud Integration unless there are connectivity or configuration issues. CamelHttpResponseCode is also sent back as 200 with ResponseCode “OK” . Failures are only visible in the response payload if the connection is fine to Azure CosmosDB.

So you need to check and verify payload after response is received in Bulk scenario.

How to troubleshoot such errors? It's another big topic and there is not place in this blog to discuss it this time but definitely you need to learn little bit on error handling on Azure side. Logs are available on AzureCosmosDB side but you need few steps and some configuration to pull out from Logs what you need. 

In our happy scenario and in any case of successful processing the response payload will give you clear information on the results for each Document processed in Bulk mode. It might be needed to analyze the response and handling problems with each Document separately, depending on scenario. 

 

Result

Let's check what is the result of our Demo iFlow on Azure CosmosDB side. We can see data injected to our container with id's set using our groovy script. The structure of JSON was accepted and CosmosDB added additional technical attributes during runtime needed to keep data in container properly. All the JSON fields that starts with "_" are CosmosDB technical attributes added during Upsert operation. They are not part of initial payload from Northing API. JSON if field from the payload is used as id key of the record/Document in Container. Using UUID "id" you can perform all CRUD operations on this Document. 

Each Document created in Container can have different JSON structures, there is no data model definition that needs to be followed. That's one of the principles of CosmosDB that is so powerful and flexible to work with. 

This demonstrates the successful replication of product data from the Northwind API to Azure CosmosDB, with the JSON structure adapted dynamically during the integration process.

Our goal is achieved, data from demo Northwind API of Products is replicated to CosmosDB using new adapter. Setup is fairly simple and testing is easy. Lets see on another options and operations available with this adapter. 

 

Other operations 

You can perform CRUD (Create, Read, Update and Delete) operation on Databases, Containers and Documents in both Single and Bulk processing mode. There are also other features that you can learn about from very good adapter documentation for example it supports operations among other as List, Query, Update, Patch Delete and Replace on various entities in Azure CosmosDB.

  • List operation: gives you easy way to get the content of each object and list databases, container and documents in containers.
  • Query operation: is quite interesting as you can inject SQL statements in your JSON request to CosmosDB and perform more advanced queries on your Documents. 
  • Update operation can be used to modify the contents of an existing Document within a
    Container in Azure CosmosDB.
  • Delete operation: can be used to delete a Database, Container, or Document in Azure
    CosmosDB.
  • Replace operation: in Azure CosmosDB involves replacing/updating multiple documents
    in a single batch operation. This is useful when you need to modify existing documents in bulk
    rather than inserting new ones.
  • Patch operation: involves path-level updates to specific files/properties to multiple
    documents in a single batch. These operations include modifications such as add, increment,
    move, remove, replace, and set.
  • Upsert operation: is used to insert or update multiple documents in a single operation. It
    updates any existing documents in CosmoDB and insert it if they are not already present.
  • Delete operation: refers to the process of deleting multiple documents in a single operation.

Below are samples configurations of adapter showing how you can List databases, Containers or Documents in Container and sample response payload. 

Sample operation 1: Get List of Databases

For this REST No-SQL query adapter provides all Databases for the given CosmosDB account. 

 

{"_rid":"","Databases":[{"id":"SampleDB","_rid":"5tgUAA==","_self":"dbs/5tgUAA==/","_etag":"\"0000c505-0000-0d00-0000-677997ed0000\"","_colls":"colls/","_users":"users/","_ts":1736021997},{"id":"ToDoList","_rid":"YCE6AA==","_self":"dbs/YCE6AA==/","_etag":"\"0000bf05-0000-0d00-0000-677997930000\"","_colls":"colls/","_users":"users/","_ts":1736021907}],"_count":2}\

 

Sample operation 2: Get List of Containers for specific Database

For this REST No-SQL query adapter provides all Containers for the give CosmosDB (below snippet is reduced for the purpose of the blog). 

 

{
	"_rid": "5tgUAA==",
	"DocumentCollections": [
		{
			"id": "Products2",
			"indexingPolicy": {
				"indexingMode": "consistent",
				"automatic": true,
				"includedPaths": [
					{
						"path": "/*"
					}
				],
				"excludedPaths": [
					{
						"path": "/\"_etag\"/?"
					}
				]
			},
			"partitionKey": {
				"paths": [
					"/id"
				],
				"kind": "Hash",
				"version": 2
			},
			"uniqueKeyPolicy": {
				"uniqueKeys": []
			},
			"conflictResolutionPolicy": {
				"mode": "LastWriterWins",
				"conflictResolutionPath": "/_ts",
				"conflictResolutionProcedure": ""
			},
			"geospatialConfig": {
				"type": "Geography"
			},
			"fullTextPolicy": {
				"defaultLanguage": "en-US",
				"fullTextPaths": []
			},
			"_rid": "5tgUAL2JYlI=",
			"_ts": 1736025012,
			"_self": "dbs/5tgUAA==/colls/5tgUAL2JYlI=/",
			"_etag": "\"0000d305-0000-0d00-0000-6779a3b40000\"",
			"_docs": "docs/",
			"_sprocs": "sprocs/",
			"_triggers": "triggers/",
			"_udfs": "udfs/",
			"_conflicts": "conflicts/",
			"computedProperties": []
		}
	],
	"_count": 2
}

 

Sample operation 3: Get List of Documents from specific Container

For this REST No-SQL query adapter provides all Documents for the given Container and Database (the snippet is reduced for the blog). 

 

{
	"_rid": "5tgUAPPYRnQ=",
	"Documents": [
		{
			"unitPrice": 13,
			"unitsInStock": 32,
			"reorderLevel": 15,
			"supplierId": 12,
			"name": "Original Frankfurter grüne Soße",
			"ProductID": 77,
			"id": "985cfdf8-43f7-4fe9-a10a-34adbab8b50a",
			"quantityPerUnit": "12 boxes",
			"discontinued": false,
			"categoryId": 2,
			"unitsOnOrder": 0,
			"_rid": "5tgUAPPYRnTnAAAAAAAAAA==",
			"_self": "dbs/5tgUAA==/colls/5tgUAPPYRnQ=/docs/5tgUAPPYRnTnAAAAAAAAAA==/",
			"_etag": "\"9a01b953-0000-0d00-0000-677ae3130000\"",
			"_attachments": "attachments/",
			"_ts": 1736106771
		}
	],
	"_count": 1
}

 

One limitation I noticed is the inability to externalize certain configuration options in the adapter. These fields must be configured directly in the iFlow, requiring a redeployment whenever changes are made. It would be beneficial to allow these options to be modified through externalized parameters in future releases. 

Summary

This blog showcases the integration of SAP Integration Suite's Azure CosmosDB receiver adapter with a practical demo scenario. It demonstrates how to pull product data from the Northwind API, transform it using Groovy scripting, and store it in Azure CosmosDB with Bulk processing. Key features of the adapter, such as CRUD operations and schema-agnostic capabilities, were explored alongside important considerations like error handling and Partition Key usage. Readers will gain insights into setting up seamless integrations for modern, hybrid-cloud applications.

Thanks for attention! Hope you enjoyed and will have fun with new Azure CosmosDB receiver adapter. 

Piotr

entry.title: #ABAP_Trial Docker image fails to start HDB
entry.content:
I have pulled the latest docker image but this fails to start HDB. My system is running Windows 11 Home with 32Gb ram and lots of free disk.

How can I diagnose the problem ?

 

docker run --stop-timeout 3600 -i --name a4h -h vhcala4hci -p 3200:3200 -p 3300:3300 -p 8443:8443 -p 30213:30213 -p 50000:50000 -p 50001:50001 sapse/abap-cloud-developer-trial:ABAPTRIAL_2022_SP01 -skip-limits-check

The SAP Developer Center Software Developer License Agreement has been accepted
because the file /agree_to_SAP_license exists
You can read the license text in the file /SAP_COMMUNITY_DEVELOPER_License
in the image.
Hint: docker exec -it a4h less /SAP_COMMUNITY_DEVELOPER_License

The file /agree_to_SAP_license exists because either you restarted a container,
or you created the file manually, or you committed the file to your private image.
If you no longer agree to SAP Developer Center Software Developer License Agreenement,
please remove the file /agree_to_SAP_license from your container or image.
Hint: docker exec -it a4h rm /agree_to_SAP_license

WARNING: the following system limits are below recommended values:

(sysctl vm.max_map_count = 262144) < 2147483647

entry.title: Hosting CodeJam in Paraguay
entry.content:
Hola Amigos!
Hello SAP Community! I hope this message finds you well. First of all, I want to congratulate you on this initiative. I participated in the 2024 SAP CodeJam in Buenos Aires and it was an incredible experience!

I would love to be the Host of the next CodeJam in Paraguay, and we have everything necessary.

  • Location: Our location is Ciudad del Este, a city that borders Iguazu Falls if you would also like to visit them. While it's not the capital, we would like to host it in this city as we have many people interested in learning new technologies. In case the logistics are complicated, we also have facilities in Asunción, the capital.
  • Requested Topic if you have a preference: We would like to explore topics such as SAP BUILD, BTP, and AI.
  • Time frame if you have something in mind: We can hold one or two sessions during the morning, from 8 AM to 1 PM. It can be on a weekday or Saturday. We have no limitations in this aspect.

Thank you very much in advance!
Greetings from Paraguay
Zami

entry.title: Re: Cloud Connector を使ってBTPとイントラネット内のAPI Gateway間でmTLS接続はできますか？
entry.content:
Markusさん、こんにちは。

日本語のままで返信失礼いたします。
いただいたヘルプページを読みました。今ヘルプページを参考にローカルでSAP Cloud Connectorを立ち上げて画面を確認しており、mTLS接続は恐らく可能だと思っています。一方で、「Cloud Connector側でアクセスするユーザを識別すること」に関してはヘルプページにも書かれていない認識なのですが、これは実現不可能なのでしょうか？

例えば以下のようなことをしたいと考えています。
・A社の人がA社用のアプリからAPIを叩く→Cloud ConnectorでA社用の証明書を使い、イントラネット内のAPI Gateway間でmTLS接続をする
・A社の人がB社用のアプリからAPIを叩く→認証を失敗にする

どうぞよろしくお願いいたします。
Moeka Matsumura

Could you please let me know if there is a way to recover them - because without them the SLR reports for all our customer systems will be impacted. Thank you!

entry.title: SAP UI5 Control for input with floating label
entry.content:
In one of our Ui5 apps in SAP BTP ,
1.we have a requirement to have a view with input fields having floating label which is shown in the attachment .When we searched the SAPUI5 SDK for the availability of this control , unfortunately we could not find one.
So here we are requesting the SAP team to guide us if there is any SAPui5 control available which we can use for the input Field with Floating label.
2.and for the Document upload is there any control which exactly matches the one which is there below in the  attached document.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: SAP UI5 Control for input with floating label
entry.content:
I could not find any information in the provided articles that directly addresses the implementation of input fields with floating labels or a specific document upload control that matches your requirements.

For floating label input fields, you might need to create a custom control or use a combination of existing controls with custom CSS and JavaScript to achieve the desired effect.

For document upload functionality, the `sap.ui.unified.FileUploader` or `sap.m.UploadCollection` controls can be used, but customization might be necessary to match the exact requirements shown in your attachment.

If you have any other questions or need further assistance, please let me know!

 

For your requirements in the SAPUI5 application on SAP BTP, here are the solutions for both the floating label input fields and the document upload control:

### 1. Floating Label Input Fields

SAPUI5 does not have a built-in control specifically named "floating label input field," but you can achieve this functionality using the `sap.m.Input` control combined with some CSS and JavaScript. Here’s how you can implement it:

#### Example Implementation:

1. **HTML (XML View)**:
   ```xml
   <mvc:View
       controllerName="your.controller.name"
       xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
       xmlns="sap.m">
       <VBox>
           <Label text="Name" class="floating-label" />
           <Input id="nameInput" placeholder=" " />
       </VBox>
   </mvc:View>
   ```

2. **CSS**:
   ```css
   .floating-label {
       position: absolute;
       pointer-events: none;
       transition: 0.2s ease all;
       -moz-transition: 0.2s ease all;
       -webkit-transition: 0.2s ease all;
       top: 10px;
       left: 10px;
       color: #757575;
   }

   .sapMInput:focus + .floating-label,
   .sapMInput:not(:placeholder-shown) + .floating-label {
       top: -20px;
       font-size: 12px;
       color: #5264AE;
   }

   .sapMInput {
       border: none;
       border-bottom: 1px solid #757575;
       outline: none;
       padding: 10px 10px 10px 0;
   }

   .sapMInput:focus {
       border-bottom: 2px solid #5264AE;
   }
   ```

3. **JavaScript (Controller)**:
   ```javascript
   sap.ui.define([
       "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
   ], function (Controller) {
       "use strict";
       return Controller.extend("your.controller.name", {
           onInit: function () {
               // Initialization code if needed
           }
       });
   });
   ```

This example uses the `sap.m.Input` control and CSS to create a floating label effect. The label will float above the input field when the input is focused or when it contains text.

### 2. Document Upload Control

For document upload functionality, SAPUI5 provides the `sap.ui.unified.FileUploader` control, which can be customized to match your requirements. Here’s how you can use it:

#### Example Implementation:

1. **HTML (XML View)**:
   ```xml
   <mvc:View
       controllerName="your.controller.name"
       xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
       xmlns="sap.ui.unified">
       <VBox>
           <FileUploader
               id="fileUploader"
               name="myFileUpload"
               uploadUrl="upload/url"
               placeholder="Choose a file for upload"
               buttonText="Browse"
               change="onFileChange"
               uploadComplete="onUploadComplete" />
       </VBox>
   </mvc:View>
   ```

2. **JavaScript (Controller)**:
   ```javascript
   sap.ui.define([
       "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
   ], function (Controller) {
       "use strict";
       return Controller.extend("your.controller.name", {
           onFileChange: function (oEvent) {
               // Handle file selection
               var oFileUploader = oEvent.getSource();
               var oFile = oEvent.getParameter("files")[0];
               // Perform any additional processing if needed
           },
           onUploadComplete: function (oEvent) {
               // Handle upload completion
               var sResponse = oEvent.getParameter("response");
               // Process the response if needed
           }
       });
   });
   ```

The `sap.ui.unified.FileUploader` control provides a comprehensive solution for file uploads, including features like file selection, upload progress, and completion handling. You can customize its appearance and behavior to match your specific requirements.

entry.title: Lambda Architecture Implementation Using SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
1. Pre-requisites

  • SAP Datasphere
  • SAP HANA Cloud Data Lake Files
  • SAP HANA Cloud Database
  • Apache Spark
  • Apache Kafka (SAP BTP Compatible)
  • SAP Analytics Cloud (Optional)

2. What is Lambda Architecture for Analytics?

Lambda architecture is designed to handle analytics for continuously changing high volume data  https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lambda_architecture . Lambda Architecture, when applied to ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) data, enables the processing and analysis of large volumes of transactional and operational data, catering to both historical and real-time analytical needs. This consists of a Batch Layer, which processes data in a batch process that runs on preset time intervals. Simultaneously, the Speed Layer handles real-time data streams, providing instant insights into live sales figures, stock levels, and other critical metrics using technologies like Kafka. The Reporting Layer merges outputs from both the Batch and Speed layers, offering a unified view of ERP data for quick access, which is essential for dashboards, ad-hoc reporting, and querying by various business applications.

Lambda Architecture

3. Setup Overview

Here S/4 HANA System shall be used as a data source and Datasphere shall be used for replicating the data from S/4 HANA System into layers. Setup overview shall look like the following..

Lambda Architecture using SAP Datasphere and SAP HANA Cloud

 

3.1 Data Source(s)

Here CDS Views of SAP S/4 HANA System are used as data sources. These should be enabled for data extraction with valid delta ( CDC delta mechanism ) setup.

 

3.2 Speed Layer

The speed layer consists of 2 components:

  1. Apache Kafka (Variant supported by SAP Datasphere has to be used here) for real-time replication of SAP S/4 HANA data from the chosen CDS Views
  2. A BTP App that shall read the Kafka Topics and apply the needed transformations and finally write the data to HANA Cloud Database.

3.3 Batch Layer

The batch layer consists of 2 components:

  1. SAP HANA Data Lake Files for storing all the data replicated from data source and also to implement medallion architecture for lakehouse ( Delta Lake).
  2. Apache Spark for transforming the raw data into the needed form for reporting ( with or without medallion architecture and delta lake )

3.4 Reporting Layer

The reporting layer consists of 2 components:

  1. SAP HANA Cloud Database for storing the data coming from speed layer as well as virtualizing / replicating the batch layer data.
  2. SAP Analytics Cloud for facilitating reporting as well as analysis

3.5 SAP Datasphere

The main component of this architecture and orchestrator of the data. Replication Flows component and spaces concept of the SAP Datasphere are employed which replicate the data from CDS Views of SAP S/4 HANA System to the chosen target.

More information on Datasphere spaces can be found here  https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/explore-sap-datasphere/introducing-sap-datasphere-spaces or https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/data-warehouse-cloud-4-spaces..html .

Currently, within a given Datasphere space, a data source (CDS View of SAP S/4 HANA System) can only be replicated to one target. This shall change once the new feature on the roadmap is delivered  https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?range=CURRENT-LAST&PRODUCT=73555000100800002141#Q1%202025;INNO=BBD86266C2A61EEF88EADB81CAE093FC .

  1. Space1: Speed Layer –
    1. Create a space for speed layer
    2. Create connection to SAP S/4 HANA System
    3. Create connection to Kafka
  2. Space2: Batch Layer –
    1. Create space for Batch layer
    2. Create connection to SAP S/4 HANA System (Datasphere connections are space specific)
    3. Create connection to SAP HANA Data Lake Files

 

4. Implementation Steps

4.1 Speed Layer

4.1.1 Create Datasphere Space for Speed Layer

Datasphere Spaces for Batch and Speed Layers

 

4.1.2 SAP S/4 HANA Connection Setup

Connection to SAP S/4 HANA OnPremise system needs Cloud Connector setup. Detailed steps are available here 

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/be5967d099974c69b77f4549425ca4c0/a49a1e3cc50f4af89711d8306bdd8f26.html
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/9f804b8efa8043539289f42f372c4862/6de74f7731c54ce88f2883df8f8671a8.html
  • https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=McRpQKx9Hzw
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/sap-datasphere-connectivity-with-s-4-hana-system-amp-sap-analytics-cloud/ba-p/13636045

Pass the connection test

 

4.1.3 Kafka Setup and Datasphere Connection

Follow the steps in the blog use SAP BTP Compliant Kafka and connect to SAP Datasphere  https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-datasphere-replication-flows-blog-series-part-3-integration-with-kafka/ba-p/13580350 .

4.1.4 Create the Replication flow for required CDS View ( I_COMPANYCODE is used here)

  1. Go to Datasphere  Data Builder  New Replication Flow.
  2. Select the Source as S/4 HANA Connection created in previous step and
  3. Target as Kafka
  4. Change Delta Load Interval to 0 Hours and 0 Minutes to make it real-time
  5. Deploy the Replication Flow and Check the metrics tab for more details upon Initial Run completion

6. Run the Replication Flow and check data in the target ( here Kafka Topic )

Check data ( Kafka Topic is created and data is replicated into the Kafka Topic)

Note:- Checking a topic details (UI or CLI ) will be different based on the chosen Kafka

4.1.5 Transform, Enrich and Write to Database

Data should be read from Kafka topic and transformed, enriched and finally the results are written to HANA Cloud Database in the corresponding “speed layer” table.

While this can be done in many ways, a simple BTP App is depicted here and detailed steps involved like data cleansing, transformation, enrichment (lookups etc.) depend on the scenario. COMPANYCODE example chosen here is a master data and not suitable for such steps and hence skipped.

 

4.2 Batch Layer

4.2.1 Create Datasphere Space for Speed Layer

 

4.2.2 SAP S/4 HANA Connection Setup

Repeat the steps of this connection from “Speed Layer” again. As mentioned earlier, this is needed until feature of multiple targets for same datasource is released   https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?range=CURRENT-LAST&PRODUCT=73555000100800002141#Q1%202025;INNO=BBD86266C2A61EEF88EADB81CAE093FC .

4.2.3 HANA Data Lake Files Connection

Provision the instance of HANA Cloud, Data Lake Files in HANA Cloud Cockpit (Relational Engine is not required)

Create a connection to HANA Cloud, Data Lake Files using the steps provided  https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/be5967d099974c69b77f4549425ca4c0/356e41e880e54255891b702d2afefeb3.html or https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/exporting-tables-from-datasphere-to-hana-datalake-file-system-using/ba-p/13892065 .

4.2.4 Create the Replication Flow for required CDS View ( I_COMPANYCODE is used here)

  • Go to Datasphere  Data Builder  New Replication Flow.
  • Select the Source as S/4 HANA Connection created in previous step and
  • Target as HANA Data Lake Files
  • Change Delta Load Interval to 24 hours and 0 minutes

  • Deploy the Replication Flow and Run the same

  • Run the Replication Flow and Monitor in Datasphere’s “Data Integration Monitor”

  • Check metrics tab for more details:

  • Check the Data in Data Lake Files using Database Explorer

 

4.2.5 Transform, Enrich and Write Results

Data Lake based analytics using Apache Spark is a huge topic with many architectures. But, Medallion lakehouse Architecture (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/lakehouse/medallion ) is suitable for S/4 HANA data as it’s a transaction data with deltas. This can be implemented using delta.io (https://delta.io/blog/delta-lake-medallion-architecture/ ) libraries directly or using databricks (https://www.databricks.com/glossary/medallion-architecture ) or other frameworks and tools. This part is skipped here.

 

4.3 Reporting Layer

Reporting layer in this scenario consists of HANA Cloud Data Lake Files (containing batch layer results as a Gold layer tables) and HANA Cloud Database (containing speed layer results).

 

4.3.1 HANA Cloud Database:

  1. Speed Layer results in Physical Table
  2. Batch Layer results in Virtual Table pointing to Gold layer tables of HANA Data Lake Files
    • An aggregated set of gold layer tables can also be stored in physical tables based on memory.

4.3.2 Data Models and Data Marts:

HANA Cloud Native Modeling can be used for developing the Calculation Views that unions the data from speed and batch layers and finally enrich the same with Calculation Views with star-join nodes.

Alternatively, Datasphere’s Views and Analytical Models in Data Builder can also be used.

 

4.3.3 Analysis using SAP Analytics Cloud

Analytical Models of Datasphere or Calculation Views of HANA Cloud developed above can be used to create Dashboards and Analysis to support the reporting.

 

5. Conclusion

Datasphere’s Connections, Replication Flows along with HANA Cloud Database and HANA Cloud Data Lake Files can be used to achieve the lambda architecture for high volume S/4 HANA Systems with real-time reporting needs.

entry.title: Re: Can I change item category of KMAT line item in VA02
entry.content: For this case, I would like to recommend you set the original item to rejected and created a new item for procurement.

entry.title: Re: What are page templates used for in Smart Edit?
entry.content:
hi @Summitt12 You're right, wcms page templates are simply the template designs of a wcsm page, it specifies the structure, layout, styles and even components on the wcms pages, which are created out of page templates. 

Please refer to the following e-learning course for more details on pages, templates, slots and components. 

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/expand-upon-technical-essentials-of-sap-commerce-cloud/determining-essential-data-models-of-web-content-management_c9595e46-4350-4fc5-89ae-2d2485260886

entry.title: Re: Query on add a property to a set of products in Commerce
entry.content:
Hi @Summitt12 

The type system and classification system are two important options of product modeling, other options are 

Basic Product Variants, Multidimensional Product Variants, Configurable Products, etc. 

Please refer to the following e-learning course for more details

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/explore-the-technical-essentials-of-sap-commerce-cloud/product-modeling-approaches_d9e3c555-eb57-4be8-bad5-b47353caff2a

WRT your query about creating a subtype of the product item type, yes it's essentially the type system option to add new properties, which requires development and redeployment efforts, but is the most runtime performant.

entry.title: Re: Nested Lists on SBPA
entry.content:
Hello @JRogerio_BTP ,

Once you imported the Action SDK to your project. You should have the data type automatically available that corresponds to your list item. 

There are two ways you can approach this:

1. You can create the data using the DataType structure from PO Action. Create a For Each to pass the data and fulfill the list.  Use the output as the input for your PO Action.

2. You can create a custom script, same approach but more of manually scripting the parameters to create a data structure as input for your PO Action.

This is a bit tricky to be honest when doing it the first time, but I hope the concept helps.

entry.title: How can I change the 'Activity Start Time' in Tcode '/N/SAPAPO/SEQ1' by using BAPI.
entry.content:
I use a bapi '/SAPAPO/RRP_LC_ORDER_CHANGE' to change this 'Activity Start Time', but it does not return any error message and 'SY_SUBRC = 0'. 'Activity Start Time' have no any change. Tcode: '/N/SAPAPO/SEQ1' 

Please help me to check the following code:

DATA: iv_order      TYPE /sapapo/orderuid,
      gv_simversion TYPE /sapapo/vrsioid,
      gt_ordkey     TYPE /sapapo/om_ordkey_tab,
      gt_order      TYPE /sapapo/om_order_ext_tab,
      gt_activities TYPE /sapapo/om_tab_act,
      gs_ordkey     TYPE /sapapo/ordkey,
      gs_order      TYPE /sapapo/om_order_ext_str.

iv_order = 'kTy6E{AL7k{dc0zXuY8X6G'.
gv_simversion = '000'.

CALL FUNCTION '/SAPAPO/RRP_LC_ORDER_GET_DATA'
  EXPORTING
    iv_order      = iv_order
    iv_simversion = gv_simversion
  IMPORTING
    es_ordkey     = gs_ordkey
    et_ordkey     = gt_ordkey
    es_order      = gs_order
    et_order      = gt_order
    et_activities = gt_activities.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  DATA: gv_method      TYPE /sapapo/om_method VALUE 'G',
        gv_application TYPE /sapapo/rrp_application VALUE 'I',
        ls_order       TYPE /sapapo/om_order,
        gt_rc          TYPE /sapapo/om_lc_rc_tab.
  DATA: iv_simid       TYPE /sapapo/vrsioid VALUE '000'.

  READ TABLE gt_activities ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_activities>)
  WITH KEY resid = '1oXmGQmT7k{9ZuMk6lKX6G'.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    <fs_activities>-startti = '20241230003104'.
  ENDIF.

  ls_order =  CORRESPONDING #( gs_order ).

  CALL FUNCTION '/SAPAPO/RRP_LC_ORDER_CHANGE'
    EXPORTING
      iv_method      = gv_method
      is_ordkey      = gs_ordkey
      it_activities  = gt_activities
      iv_application = gv_application
      iv_flush       = abap_true
    IMPORTING
      et_rc          = gt_rc
    EXCEPTIONS
      error_in_opr   = 1
      invalid_params = 2
      unknown_error  = 3
      OTHERS         = 4.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    CALL FUNCTION '/SAPAPO/SEQM_RRP_COMMIT'
      EXCEPTIONS
        commit_error = 1
        OTHERS       = 2.

    CALL FUNCTION 'RPM_FCODE_SAVE'
      EXPORTING
        iv_simid    = iv_simid
        iv_popup    = space
        iv_show_msg = space
      EXCEPTIONS
        fatal_error = 1
        other_error = 2
        OTHERS      = 3.

    COMMIT WORK AND WAIT .
    CALL FUNCTION '/SAPAPO/RRP_COMMIT'.
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.

entry.title: Re: RE-FX Intereses
entry.content:
Hi just responding in English so it is easier for other people to comment.

There are many factors that impact the interest calculation. Normally the frequency term on the valuation rule the payment term is set to "In arrears". Also in the valuation rule config there is a drop down regarding the "Interest calculation method". This will also have an impact. Look at the F1 help text for further information.

Also very often what the customer has been using is not necessarily correct so that might require an adjustment in the ledger as the interest is fully calculated in RE-FX.

Regarding questions #2 have a look at KBA note 2544232 - Leasing: Handling transition scenarios. This will provide a lot of information on the options and prerequisites of handling contract migrations.

Regards, Andre

entry.title: Re: RE-FX Intereses
entry.content: Thank you so much for your help! I will look into the configuration of the valuation rule to see if I can arrive at the interest amount I need or, alternatively, give the user the option to manually adjust these values. Regarding point 2, Scenario 1: Transfer of finance leases that existed before the application date matches my scenario, so I will propose the detailed steps. Thanks again for this, it was very helpful!

entry.title: Need info regarding DB FUll backup
entry.content:
Dear SAP Team,


Kindly check below details and please provide us solution ASAP.


1) we are scheduling DB full backup on our resiliency server for ever alternative weeks, when DB backup scheduled, in IQMSG logs total number of IQ blocks to be backed up and total number of IQ blocks backed up records are matching, but same entry cannot be seen in sysiqbackuphistory table, where as we are daily refreshing resiliency DB through full virtual decoupled and incremental since full backup which are getting logged in the sysiqbackuphistory table and since the every alternative week post the resiliency DB refresh we are manually scheduling DB full backup in the database hence this would be the reason where it cannot be logged in sysiqbackuphistory table and kindly let us know is there a way that we can make it logged to this sysiqbackuphistory table?


 


2) Since in IQ it will not be written like explicitly backup started in backup completed, Is there any configuration or a statement or print statement which we can include in our script which will explicitly write into IQMSG or SRV logs where backup has started or backup has completed statements.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Need info regarding DB FUll backup
entry.content:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1) we are scheduling DB full backup on our resiliency server for ever alternative weeks, when DB backup scheduled, in IQMSG logs total number of IQ blocks to be backed up and total number of IQ blocks backed up records are matching, but same entry cannot be seen in sysiqbackuphistory table, where as we are daily refreshing resiliency DB through full virtual decoupled and incremental since full backup which are getting logged in the sysiqbackuphistory table and since the every alternative week post the resiliency DB refresh we are manually scheduling DB full backup in the database hence this would be the reason where it cannot be logged in sysiqbackuphistory table and kindly let us know is there a way that we can make it logged to this sysiqbackuphistory table?
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
==>
"sysiqbackuphistory" does not have any information regarding "Total blocks to backup".
You may find "Total blocks to backup" from "sysiqbackuphistorydetail.num_blocks_backed_up".
However, please note that "sysiqbackuphistorydetail.num_blocks_backed_up" may show number of blocks backed up by IQ engine only.
If dbfiles for IQ Main Stores are backed up by O/S command in case of IQ virtual backup, then IQ cannot check the exact number of blocks backed up by the O/S command.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2) Since in IQ it will not be written like explicitly backup started in backup completed, Is there any configuration or a statement or print statement which we can include in our script which will explicitly write into IQMSG or SRV logs where backup has started or backup has completed statements
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
==>
Please refer to KBA 2285840 for more detail information regarding start/complete information regarding IQ backups from .iqmsg file.

--2285840 - How To Determine Backup Progress in SAP IQ
https://me.sap.com/notes/2285840

entry.title: Possible to replicate deprecated oAuth SF authentication using SAP Cloud Identity to SuccessFactors?
entry.content:
With the deprecation of OAuth IdP API in SuccessFactors, I'm looking to see if I can do the same thing with SAP Cloud Identity Services.

In short, SuccessFactors has a Todo api that I can use to get the total Todo's that I can display in the Fiori Launchpad but this API is user specific (cannot be executed by a dedicated API user). There is another v2 API that allows filtering by user but it does not work for all Todo types so not an option.

So using the Todo API was fine with the deprecated OAuth as it allowed me, via client id/secret to get a SAML token for any employee to call this API. 

So first question - Is this possible within SAP Cloud Identity?

Second question - I found this link discussed something similar but I'm wondering do I just need a single SAML2 Application created that can give me an access token, which I can convert to SAML2 to authenticate to SuccessFactors?

Lastly, how do I specify the user within the client request without password and get the SAML token to use for SuccessFactors?

e.g. Firstly, I posted to /oauth2/token with grant_type: client_credentials and client_id: "User id I want to log in as"? - with Basic Auth using client id and secret - but this doesn't seem to check the user-id, so pretty sure I'm doing this first step wrong?

If it is working, then I need to convert this to SAML2 via grant_type: urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange...

A little lost, so appreciate any help.

Thanks,
Matt
